description: Given a string, find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters.

description: Merge two sorted linked lists into one sorted linked list.

description: Determine if a linked list has a cycle using O(1) space.

title: Find the middle of a linked list.
description: Find the middle node of a singly linked list.

title: Valid parentheses.
description: Given a string containing '(', ')', '{', '}', '[', ']', determine if the input string is valid.

title: What is Big O notation?
description: What does Big O notation describe?
options: Describes the upper bound of an algorithm's time/space complexity as input grows, focusing on the worst case
correct_answer: Describes the upper bound of an algorithm's time/space complexity as input grows, focusing on the worst case

title: What is the time complexity of binary search?
description: What is the time complexity of binary search?
options: O(log n) — halves the search space with each comparison
correct_answer: O(log n) — halves the search space with each comparison

title: What is a hash table?
description: How does a hash table work?
options: Maps keys to values using a hash function; provides O(1) average-case lookup, insertion, and deletion
correct_answer: Maps keys to values using a hash function; provides O(1) average-case lookup, insertion, and deletion

title: What is the difference between BFS and DFS?
description: How do BFS and DFS differ?
options: BFS explores level by level using a queue; DFS explores as deep as possible using a stack/recursion
correct_answer: BFS explores level by level using a queue; DFS explores as deep as possible using a stack/recursion

title: What is dynamic programming?
description: What is dynamic programming?
options: A technique that solves complex problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing results to avoid recomputation (memoization/tabulation)
correct_answer: A technique that solves complex problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing results to avoid recomputation (memoization/tabulation)

title: What is the difference between memoization and tabulation?
description: How do top-down and bottom-up DP differ?
options: Memoization (top-down) uses recursion + cache; tabulation (bottom-up) fills a table iteratively from base cases
correct_answer: Memoization (top-down) uses recursion + cache; tabulation (bottom-up) fills a table iteratively from base cases

title: What is a balanced BST?
description: What makes a binary search tree balanced?
options: A BST where the height difference between left and right subtrees of every node is at most 1, ensuring O(log n) operations
All leaves are at the same level
It has equal left and right nodes
correct_answer: A BST where the height difference between left and right subtrees of every node is at most 1, ensuring O(log n) operations

title: What is a heap?
description: What is a binary heap?
options: A complete binary tree where each parent is greater than (max-heap) or less than (min-heap) its children; supports O(log n) insert/extract and O(1) peek
correct_answer: A complete binary tree where each parent is greater than (max-heap) or less than (min-heap) its children; supports O(log n) insert/extract and O(1) peek

title: What is the two-pointer technique?
description: How does the two-pointer technique work?
options: Uses two pointers (often at start/end or slow/fast) that move toward each other or at different speeds to solve problems in O(n) time
correct_answer: Uses two pointers (often at start/end or slow/fast) that move toward each other or at different speeds to solve problems in O(n) time

title: What is the sliding window technique?
description: How does sliding window work?
options: Maintains a window (subarray/substring) that expands or shrinks as it slides over the input, solving range-based problems in O(n)
correct_answer: Maintains a window (subarray/substring) that expands or shrinks as it slides over the input, solving range-based problems in O(n)

title: What is a graph?
description: What is a graph data structure?
options: A collection of nodes (vertices) connected by edges; can be directed/undirected, weighted/unweighted, cyclic/acyclic
correct_answer: A collection of nodes (vertices) connected by edges; can be directed/undirected, weighted/unweighted, cyclic/acyclic

title: What is Dijkstra's algorithm?
description: What does Dijkstra's algorithm find?
options: Finds the shortest path from a source to all other vertices in a weighted graph with non-negative edges; uses a priority queue
correct_answer: Finds the shortest path from a source to all other vertices in a weighted graph with non-negative edges; uses a priority queue

title: What is topological sort?
description: What is topological sorting?
options: A linear ordering of vertices in a DAG such that for every edge u→v, u comes before v; used for dependency resolution
correct_answer: A linear ordering of vertices in a DAG such that for every edge u→v, u comes before v; used for dependency resolution

title: What is a monotonic stack?
description: What is a monotonic stack?
options: A stack that maintains elements in strictly increasing or decreasing order; useful for next greater element, histogram problems
correct_answer: A stack that maintains elements in strictly increasing or decreasing order; useful for next greater element, histogram problems

title: What is Union-Find (Disjoint Set)?
description: What is the Union-Find data structure?
options: A data structure that tracks elements partitioned into disjoint sets with near O(1) union and find operations using path compression and union by rank
correct_answer: A data structure that tracks elements partitioned into disjoint sets with near O(1) union and find operations using path compression and union by rank

title: What is backtracking?
description: What is the backtracking technique?
options: An algorithmic technique that builds solutions incrementally, abandoning partial solutions (backtracking) when they cannot lead to a valid solution
correct_answer: An algorithmic technique that builds solutions incrementally, abandoning partial solutions (backtracking) when they cannot lead to a valid solution

title: What is the time complexity of quicksort?
description: Average and worst case of quicksort.
options: Average: O(n log n), Worst: O(n²) when pivot selections are poor; randomized pivot helps avoid worst case
correct_answer: Average: O(n log n), Worst: O(n²) when pivot selections are poor; randomized pivot helps avoid worst case

title: What is amortized analysis?
description: What does amortized analysis measure?
options: The average time per operation over a worst-case sequence of operations; e.g., dynamic array append is O(1) amortized despite occasional O(n) resizing
correct_answer: The average time per operation over a worst-case sequence of operations; e.g., dynamic array append is O(1) amortized despite occasional O(n) resizing

title: What is a segment tree?
description: What is a segment tree used for?
options: A tree for efficient range queries (sum, min, max) and point/range updates in O(log n) time
correct_answer: A tree for efficient range queries (sum, min, max) and point/range updates in O(log n) time

title: What is the difference between a stack and a queue?
description: How do stack and queue differ?
options: Stack is LIFO (Last In, First Out); Queue is FIFO (First In, First Out)
correct_answer: Stack is LIFO (Last In, First Out); Queue is FIFO (First In, First Out)

title: What is a priority queue?

correct_answer: A queue where elements are dequeued based on priority (highest/lowest), typically implemented with a heap
